coredata.dc:title: Waste toner–derived carbon dots for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Dajin
Park, Wonchan
Kim, Hyemin
Park, Yoonsang
Byun, Yerim
Cho, Soohyun
Hahn, Sei Kwang
Han, Hyunho
Kwon, Woosung
coredata.dc:description: Printer toner, which accounts for a large fraction of electrical and electronic wastes, poses serious environmental and health risks but cannot be efficiently recycled because of its high chemical durability and complexity. Specifically, only 20–30% of waste toner is recycled, while much of the remainder ends up in landfills and thus contributes to air/soil pollution and respiratory disease development. Herein, we report the one-pot hydrothermal processing of waste toner into well-dispersed and uniform-sized carbon dots featuring abundant surface defects (e.g., radicals and dangling bonds), strongly interacting with UV (200–400 nm) light, and exhibiting intense photoluminescence in both UV (300 nm) and visible (400–500 nm) ranges. In particular, ethanolamine is used to chemically disintegrate the carbon black cores of toner particles into nanoparticles and simultaneously passivate the nanoparticle surface. According to the results of time-correlated single photon counting spectroscopy measurements, UV- and visible-region photoluminescence originates from amorphous carbon structures in the core and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface, respectively. Finally, in view of their excellent visible-region optical properties and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species under laser irradiation, the waste toner–derived carbon dots are demonstrated to be promising agents for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste toner
Recycling
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal process
Bioimaging
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of wt-CDs
Bioimaging and PDT applications of wt-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic chemical structure of TPs and (b) the synthesis of wt-CDs.

SEM images of (a) pristine and (b) ethanol-treated TPs. (c) TEM image of wt-CDs. Raman spectra of (d) pristine TPs, (e) ethanol-treated TPs, and (f) wt-CDs. XRD patterns of (g) pristine TPs, (h) ethanol-treated TPs, and (i) wt-CDs.
High-resolution (a, b, c) C 1s and (d, e, f) N 1s spectra of (a, d) pristine TPs, (b, e) ethanol-treated TPs, and (c, f) wt-CDs. (g) FT-IR spectra of pristine TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption (yellow) and PLE spectra (blue, λ em = 450 nm) of wt-CDs. (b) PL contour map of wt-CDs. (c) PL spectra of wt-CDs. (d) TCSPC signals of wt-CDs within the PL time range.

(a) Cytotoxicity of wt-CDs to A549 (yellow) and PC3 (blue) cells (n = 5). (b) Confocal microscopy images of PC3 cells (b) before and (c) after treatment with wt-CDs. Cells treated with wt-CDs showed a blue signal, which was not observed in the control group. The plasma membranes were stained with Alexa Fluor™ 488-conjugated wheat germ agglutinin (green).

(a) PL spectra of wt-CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) after exposure to UV radiation (254 nm) in the presence of SOSG for 0–2 h. (b) PDT test of PC3 cells (n = 4) exposed to different concentrations of wt-CDs. (c) Live/dead assay confocal microscopy images of PC3 cells exposed to wt-CD concentrations of 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1 (from left to right).
Elemental analysis data of TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs.
Photophysical properties and PL lifetimes of wt-CDs.

head.ce:title: Waste toner–derived carbon dots for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Printer toner, which accounts for a large fraction of electrical and electronic wastes, poses serious environmental and health risks but cannot be efficiently recycled because of its high chemical durability and complexity. Specifically, only 20–30% of waste toner is recycled, while much of the remainder ends up in landfills and thus contributes to air/soil pollution and respiratory disease development. Herein, we report the one-pot hydrothermal processing of waste toner into well-dispersed and uniform-sized carbon dots featuring abundant surface defects (e.g., radicals and dangling bonds), strongly interacting with UV (200–400 nm) light, and exhibiting intense photoluminescence in both UV (300 nm) and visible (400–500 nm) ranges. In particular, ethanolamine is used to chemically disintegrate the carbon black cores of toner particles into nanoparticles and simultaneously passivate the nanoparticle surface. According to the results of time-correlated single photon counting spectroscopy measurements, UV- and visible-region photoluminescence originates from amorphous carbon structures in the core and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface, respectively. Finally, in view of their excellent visible-region optical properties and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species under laser irradiation, the waste toner–derived carbon dots are demonstrated to be promising agents for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The proliferation of printers has resulted in the accumulation of large quantities of toner powder waste, which poses significant environmental and health risks and accounts for a large fraction of electrical and electronic waste materials [1–4]. Toner powder is designed to be sufficiently stable to withstand printing conditions and is therefore difficult to recycle. Globally, only 20–30% of waste toner is recycled, while most of the remainder is dumped in landfills and thus exacerbates air/soil pollution and respiratory illnesses [5–7]. Therefore, numerous studies on the recycling of waste toner, including those on its transformation into valuable products, have been conducted to overcome the inherent chemical resilience and complexity of this material [1,3,8]. The existing methods of waste toner processing include recombination with carbon black nanoparticles to remanufacture toner powder [1,9] and the recovery of pure iron [1,10].

The recent years have witnessed the emergence of novel strategies utilizing the compositional properties of waste toner for nanomaterial synthesis [11–14]. Toner powder is a hybrid organic-inorganic nanocomposite primarily containing granulated plastic (mostly polystyrene or polyester derivatives), carbon black (CB), iron oxide, and several other additives responsible for special effects such as coloring and triboelectrification. Among these components, CB is of particular interest for toner recycling because of its high usability and applicability in numerous industrial fields such as mechanics, electronics, optoelectronics, and catalysis [15–18]. CB is a powdered form of carbon typically containing spherical and partially graphitic particles with a regularity lower than that of graphite [19,20] and can be readily transformed into other carbon nanostructures such as carbon nanoparticles, nanotubes, and nanocomposites because of its high content of structural and chemical defects that can serve as reaction sites [19–21].

Among such carbon nanostructures, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied as new multifunctional carbon nanomaterials for a variety of applications ranging from biology to optoelectronics. In particular, CDs have great potential for biosensing, bioimaging, therapy, drug delivery, and other biomedical applications because of their unique optical characteristics, relatively low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [22–28]. Herein, we describe a simple one-pot solvothermal method for the efficient recycling of toner powder waste through its disintegration into CDs under the action of ethanolamine. In particular, ethanolamine facilitates the chemical breakdown of CB contained in toner powder into nanoparticles while simultaneously passivating their surface to afford well-dispersed size-uniform waste toner–derived CDs (wt-CDs) displaying strong UV absorption and two distinct photoluminescence (PL) emissions originating from carbon-core-state and surface-state transitions. PL decay spectroscopy and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation measurements are used to elucidate the potential pathway of radiative/nonradiative transitions/transfers of photoexcited electrons in wt-CDs. Finally, wt-CDs are used as agents for in vitro imaging and photodynamic therapy (PDT) to demonstrate the practical value of our method and its potential for application in toner powder waste recycling platforms.

Toner powder (black) that had been employed in Samsung and Hewlett Packard toner cartridges was purchased from Jeil and Entobeto (Seoul, Korea). Ethanolamine (≥98%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ethanol (anhydrous, 95%) was purchased from Samchun Chemicals (Seoul, Korea). All chemicals were used as received without further purification or modification.

Toner powder (100 mg) was dispersed in ethanol (11 mL) upon 10-min stirring, and the solution was supplemented with ethanolamine (0.6036 mL or 0.01 mol), transferred into a stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 220 °C for 4 h. The resulting black liquid was naturally cooled to room temperature and filtered through a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) syringe filter (hydrophilic, 0.2 μm) to remove residual toner powder. The yellow filtrate was dialyzed against ethanol for at least three days using Spectra/Por Biotech cellulose ester dialysis tubes (Spectrum Chemical, Gardena, CA, USA) (100–500 Da). Ethanol was removed by rotary evaporation, and the residue was redispersed in water (4 mg mL−1) for further use.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging was performed on a JEOL JSM-7600F instrument (accelerating voltage = 20 kV). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was performed on a JEM-2200FS instrument (accelerating voltage = 200 kV) equipped with a Cs corrector. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was performed using a Horiba SZ-100 particle size analyzer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a VG Scientific Escalab 250 spectrometer with an Al X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflection accessory.

Optical property measurements were performed using 10 × 10 mm2 QS-grade quartz cuvettes (111-QS, Hellma Analytics). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Scinco S-3100 spectrophotometer and PL spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer. In time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurements, which were performed using a HORIBA Fluorolog-3 instrument, the emitted photon signal was spectrally dispersed by a monochromator and then collected using a fast photon multiplier tube detector. Absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) were measured using a Jasco ILF835 integrating sphere (100 mm) and Jasco Spectra Manager II Version 2 software.

The toxicity of wt-CDs to human prostate cancer (PC3) and adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial (A549) cells was evaluated using the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Cells were suspended in RPMI 1640 supplemented with 1 vol% penicillin/streptomycin and 10 vol% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1, and each well of a 96-well cell culture plate was seeded with 100 μL of this suspension and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, wt-CDs were dispersed in RPMI 1640 at concentrations of 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1, and the dispersions (100 μL) were added to each well. The cell culture plates were incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator for 2 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and treated with 10 vol% CCK-8 assay solution. After 1-h incubation, the optical density of each well at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA, USA).

Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), dihydrorhodamine (DHR) 123 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), and Red Fluorometric Intracellular ROS Kit (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were used to detect singlet oxygen, hydrogen peroxide, and superoxide/hydroxyl radicals, respectively. The SOSG reagent was dissolved in 33 μL of methanol at a concentration of 5 mM. Then, 4 μL of the resulting stock solution was added to 10 mL of a standard pH 7 phosphate buffer (Daejung Chemicals & Metals, Siheung, Korea). DHR 123 was dissolved in water at a concentration of 20 μM. For the Red Fluorometric Intracellular ROS Kit, the ROS detection reagent was reconstituted with 40 μL of DMSO, and 20 μL of the resulting stock solution was added to a 10 mL assay buffer solution. The wt-CDs were dispersed in 2 mL of this solution at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 in a quartz cuvette. After a 2-h exposure to a UV lamp (254 nm, 8 W), the PL intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 504 nm and emission wavelengths (λem) of 515–600 nm for the SOSG and DHR 123 probes. The PL intensity of the Red Fluorometric Intracellular ROS Kit probe was measured at λex = 520 nm and λem = 530–700 nm.

PC3 cells were prepared at a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1 in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 1 vol% penicillin/streptomycin and 10 vol% FBS, seeded (200 μL) in each well of a chambered coverslip with eight wells, and incubated for 24 h. The medium was replaced with 200 μL of a fresh medium containing wt-CDs (1 mg mL−1), and the cells were incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS (2×), fixed with 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde solution for 15 min, incubated with 500-fold diluted Alexa Fluor™ 488-conjugated wheat germ agglutinin in Dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DPBS) for 15 min at room temperature, washed with PBS (3×), and mounted using an antifade mountant. Cellular imaging was performed using a 405-nm diode laser and a 488-nm argon laser, and the emissions from wt-CDs and Alexa Fluor™ 488 were detected at 415–475 nm and 495–535 nm, respectively.

The PDT characteristics of wt-CDs were evaluated using PC3 cells. The cells were cultured in a 96-well plate under the conditions used for the cell viability test. After 24 h, each well was supplemented with wt-CDs at concentrations of 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1, and the cell culture plate was incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator for 24 h. The cells were irradiated with a 473-nm laser at an intensity of 15 mW cm−2 for 10 min and then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After washing the cells and replacing the medium with 100 μL of a serum-free medium, 10 μL of the CCK-8 assay solution was added. Following 2-h incubation, the optical density of each well was determined at 450 nm by a microplate reader.

The live/dead assay was carried out using PC3 cells. Each well of a 96-well black cell culture plate was seeded with 104 cells, incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator for 24 h, supplemented with wt-CDs at 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1, and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were irradiated with a 473-nm laser at an intensity of 15 mW cm−2 for 10 min and incubated for 24 h. After three-fold washing with PBS, the medium was replaced by 100 μL of PBS containing 2 μM calcein AM and 4 μM ethidium homodimer-1, and the cells were incubated at room temperature for 30 min and observed by confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of wt-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Although toner formulations vary from manufacturer to manufacturer, black toner is traditionally made of plastic (or polymer) particles pigmented with CB [1,14,29]. Herein, we aimed to use this CB as a carbon source for CD production, as it is prone to chemical modification with a wide range of molecules because of its high surface-area-to-volume ratio and highly reactive surface functional groups (mainly alcohols and carbonyls) or defects (Fig. 1 a) [20,30,31]. In our approach, black toner particles (TPs) were solvothermally treated with ethanolamine in ethanol at 220 °C. According to the proposed mechanism, ethanol first removed the plastic matrices and other molecular ingredients of TPs at high temperature to extract CB, and ethanolamine then chemically disintegrated the extracted CB into wt-CDs by bonding with structurally vulnerable surface defect sites to form amino and amide groups. Residuals including unreacted molecules and ions were then removed through dialysis against ethanol. This process, namely the ethanol-based thermochemical peeling followed by ethanolamine-based chemical fragmentation, is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1b [32].

The morphology and size distribution of pristine TPs were investigated by SEM and DLS. SEM imaging (Fig. 2a and S1) revealed that TPs had an irregular shape with a mean diameter of ∼5 μm and a relatively broad size distribution. DLS data showed that TP size was mostly in the range of 2–5 μm (Fig. S2). To examine the effect of ethanol on TPs at high temperature, we treated TPs with ethanol at the synthesis temperature (220 °C). SEM imaging (Fig. 2b and S3) and DLS measurements (Fig. S2) revealed that this treatment decreased TP size by 80% (i.e., to ∼0.7 μm), demonstrating that ethanol efficiently dissolved the plastic matrix of TPs to expose CB surfaces. These surfaces subsequently engaged in chemical bonding with ethanolamine and were broken down into wt-CDs. The structure of wt-CDs was examined by TEM and compared with that of TPs. TEM imaging (Fig. 2c and S4) showed that wt-CDs had an almost spherical shape and a very narrow size distribution (8–10 nm). DLS measurements suggested that wt-CDs were uniformly distributed without any agglomeration and had an average size of 8.6 nm, in line with the results of TEM analysis (Fig. S2). High-resolution TEM imaging demonstrated that wt-CDs were polycrystalline or partially graphitic and thus probably originated directly from CB with a disordered graphitic carbon structure (Fig. S4) [19,20].

The Raman spectra of pristine TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs revealed the peaks of both graphitic (1560 cm−1, G) and amorphous (i.e., disordered (D); 1350 cm−1) carbon (Fig. 2 d–f) [33]. The D/G intensity ratios of pristine TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs (0.93, 0.90, and 0.86, respectively) suggested that these materials were composed of disordered graphitic carbon, similar to CB. The decrease in the D/G ratio upon going from pristine TPs to wt-CDs indicated that chemical treatment eliminated disordered sites, with ethanol melting off polymeric structures and ethanolamine reducing the content of defective carbon structures through amidation [32].

XRD analysis revealed the polycrystalline nature of pristine TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs (Fig. 2g–i). The XRD pattern of pristine TPs exhibited a distinct, intense carbon (002) peak (2θ ≈ 17°), along with a relatively weaker and broader carbon (101) peak (2θ ≈ 43°), indicative of varying degrees of amorphousness and graphitic ordering, respectively [34–37]. Based on the above, we concluded that pristine TPs contained loosely interconnected graphitic substructures or turbostratic carbon (a mixture of amorphous and graphitic carbon) structures [38]. The pattern of ethanol-treated TPs resembled that of pristine TPs because the polymeric structures removed using ethanol were completely amorphous. In the case of wt-CDs, the carbon (002) peak sharpened and shifted to ∼22°, while the carbon (101) peak exhibited reduced intensity. Thus, we concluded that TPs underwent chemical disintegration and significant size reduction to yield wt-CDs, limiting the graphitic ordering within their turbostratic carbon structure [35,39].

The XRD patterns were further convoluted to simulate the limited experimental resolution. Previous literatures [36,41] suggest that an increase in the distances between the carbon (002) planes (d 002) indicates a transition towards amorphous carbon. Following ethanolamine-induced thermochemical fragmentation of CBs in TPs, the d 002 value in Table S1 decreased from 5.0082 Å (TPs) to 4.0621 Å (wt-CDs), although it remained greater than that of graphite (3.4621 Å) [36]. This suggests that the relatively larger oxygen atoms in CBs were partially eliminated via a reduction reaction with ethanolamine during thermochemical fragmentation, possibly resulting in a decreased degree of amorphousness (or an increased crystallinity) and a decreased average interstructural distance between graphitic substructures closer to graphite [35,36,40,41]. In addition, the d 101 value for TPs was 2.0976 Å, whereas this value could not be determined for wt-CDs. This result suggests that ethanolamine-induced fragmentation led to a substantial decrease in size, which in turn limited the extent of graphitic ordering. This demonstrates the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the turbostratic carbon structure of CBs, highlighting the crucial role of ethanolamine in the chemical disintegration and substantial size reduction of CBs to produce wt-CDs. In conjunction with the subsequent chemical analysis of wt-CDs, the structural changes caused by ethanolamine-induced fragmentation will be discussed further.

To gain insight into the chemical properties and elemental composition of wt-CDs, XPS analysis was performed. The C 1s spectra of TPs (Fig. 3 a), ethanol-treated TPs (Fig. 3b), and wt-CDs (Fig. 3c) exhibit the characteristic C–C/C=C (284.8 eV) peak of carbonaceous materials. Following treatment with ethanolamine, the intensity of the C=O (289.0 eV) and C–O (286.5 eV) peaks in TPs and ethanol-treated TPs decreased, while the N–C=O (288.2 eV) and C–N (286.2 eV) peaks appeared in wt-CDs. This suggests that ethanolamine chemically binds to the oxygenated functional groups (or defects) of CB in TPs. The N 1s spectra of pristine TPs (Fig. 3d) and ethanol-treated TPs (Fig. 3e) displayed low nitrogen content, whereas wt-CDs (Fig. 3f) demonstrated a substantial presence of nitrogen. This confirms the crucial role of ethanolamine in the fragmentation of TPs into wt-CDs.

Elemental analysis results (Table 1 and Fig. S5) revealed that the carbon content of wt-CDs (83.9 at%) surpassed that of pristine TPs (56.2 at%) and ethanol-treated TPs (82.47 at%), indicating the successful removal of plastic matrices and possibly other components from TPs during thermochemical peeling with ethanol to extract CB. The oxygen content of wt-CDs (11.9 at%) was lower than that of TPs (41.5 at%) and ethanol-treated TPs (17.22 at%). This result is consistent with XRD data demonstrating a decrease in d 002 after chemical fragmentation, indicating the removal of oxygen atoms from the turbostratic carbon structure of TPs, which could result in a decrease in the average interstructural distance between graphitic subregions [41]. Additionally, the nitrogen content increased after ethanolamine-induced fragmentation (TPs: 1.1 at%, ethanol-treated TPs: 0.31 at%; wt-CDs: 4.2 at%). This result is also consistent with XRD data showing a reduction in the intensity of the carbon (101) peak (with an undeterminable d 101 value) after chemical fragmentation, indicating the inclusion of nitrogen atoms into the turbostratic structure of TPs, inducing their fragmentation and reducing graphitic ordering.

Fig. 3g presents the FT-IR spectra of pristine TPs, ethanol-treated TPs, and wt-CDs. As expected, the spectra of pristine and ethanol-treated TPs were similar, which confirms that ethanol treatment did not significantly change the chemical structure of TPs. The N–H stretching (3300 cm−1), amide C=O stretching (1650 cm−1), N–H bending (1550 cm−1), and C–N stretching (1070 cm−1) peaks were only observed for wt-CDs, which indicates that ethanolamine was chemically bonded to the oxygenated functional groups of CB in TPs to predominantly form amide groups. In addition, the alcohol O–H stretch (3000–2850 cm−1) was more pronounced for wt-CDs than for TPs, indicating that ethanolamine was successfully grafted at the surface of wt-CDs, in accordance with XPS results. Importantly, the hydrophilic OH groups on the surface of wt-CDs increase their stability in aqueous media, which is essential for biomedical applications.

Fig. 4 a presents the UV–vis absorption spectrum of wt-CDs. Two strong and sharp absorption peaks were detected in the deep UV region at 200 and 250 nm, corresponding to π→π* transitions in graphitic (or polyaromatic) and oxygenic carbon (mostly carbonyl) structures, respectively [39,42]. Figs. S6 and S7 demonstrate that pristine and ethanol-treated TPs also exhibited these deep-UV absorption peaks in a nearly identical wavelength region, which indicates that both TPs and wt-CDs featured graphitic/oxygenic carbon cores that were not completely destroyed through chemical treatment.

PL excitation (PLE) spectroscopy was utilized to further investigate the n→π* transition in a lower energy (longer wavelength) region caused by the surface incorporation of nitrogen via ethanolamine-induced fragmentation [43,44]. The PLE spectra of pristine and ethanol-treated TPs measured at λ em = 450 nm demonstrated a common peak at ∼300 nm, which corresponds to an n→π* transition due to their intrinsic oxygenic (mostly carbonyl) structures (Figs. S6 and S7). In contrast, the PLE spectrum of wt-CDs measured at λ em = 450 nm exhibited not only the 300 nm peak typically observed in TPs but also an additional peak at 355 nm, which corresponds to another n→π* transition due to surface nitrogen incorporated through ethanolamine-induced fragmentation (Fig. 4a and Table 1). Thus, the incorporation of surface nitrogen played a significant role in facilitating relatively long-wavelength (blue, 355 nm) absorption and the associated n→π* transition.

The PL emission contour map of wt-CDs (Fig. 4b) displayed a sharp PL peak and a broad PL band at ∼300 nm (UV) and 450 nm (blue), indicating the presence of two types of emission states with distinct origins. Specifically, UV PL occurred for 265-nm excitation corresponding to the 250-nm absorption associated with the core graphitic/oxygenic carbon structures (the core states), while blue PL occurred for 375-nm excitation corresponding to the 355-nm PLE associated with the surface nitrogenated functional groups (surface states). Fig. 4c depicts the PL emission spectra of wt-CDs measured at λ ex = 265 and 375 nm, demonstrating the excitation-dependent PL characteristics due to the presence of two distinct emission states. For 265-nm excitation, the PL emission spectrum displayed both a sharp UV PL peak and a relatively broad blue PL band, indicating that some photoexcited electrons were transferred from high-energy core states to low-energy surface states. In addition, because of the diversity of the surface chemical structures, surface states with slightly different energy levels were generated, resulting in broad PL emission [45].

To examine the decay kinetics of photoexcited carriers in wt-CDs, we performed TCSPC measurements at probe wavelengths (λ probe) of 300 and 450 nm, which corresponded to core and surface emission states, respectively (Fig. 4d and Table 2 ). The decay curve measured at λ ex = 264 nm was fitted with a monoexponential function, which indicated that the core states had a single dominant recombination pathway. This decay curve corresponded to core-state emission with a PL lifetime of 5.30 ns. Meanwhile, a biexponential function was used to fit the decay curve measured at λ ex = 374 nm, which indicated the presence of surface states with slightly different energy levels. In view of surface carrier trapping, the PL lifetime of this surface-state emission (5.75 ns) was ∼10% longer than that of the core-state emission [45–47]. Based on the results of PL and TCSPC analyses, we concluded that wt-CDs featured two different (core and surface) emission states and that the related differences in PL emission wavelengths and lifetimes were primarily due to different chemical structures and compositions. Surface carrier trapping is known to be the main factor increasing the nonradiative to radiative recombination ratio [46,47]. Indeed, PLQYs of 34% and 16% were measured at λ ex = 264 nm (core state) and 374 nm (surface state), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging and PDT applications of wt-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In view of their optical properties in UV and visible regions, wt-CDs were utilized in bioimaging and PDT. Fig. 5 a presents the toxicity of wt-CDs to A549 and PC3 cells determined using the CCK-8 assay. The viability of wt-CD-treated cells exceeded 90% at concentrations up to 1 mg mL−1, which demonstrated the high biocompatibility of wt-CDs. The confocal microscopy images of wt-CD-treated PC3 cells (Fig. 5c) showed intracellular fluorescence from wt-CDs (blue) that was not observed in the control group (Fig. 5b), inside the cell membrane stained with a fluorescently labeled wheat germ agglutinin (green). These results indicated that wt-CDs can be uptaken by cells and exhibit PL emission inside them, thus holding great promise as bioimaging agents.

We subsequently confirmed the capacity of our wt-CDs to produce ROS and identified its potential types using the SOSG assay, DHR 123, and Red Fluorometric Intracellular ROS Kit (Fig. 6a and S8). Firstly, the generation of singlet oxygen can be detected using the SOSG assay, in which the SOSG reagent is oxidized by singlet oxygen, resulting in a fluorescent derivative that emits green PL at approximately 525 nm. Secondly, we used DHR 123 to detect hydrogen peroxide generation, which emits strong green PL at 526 nm when reacted with hydrogen peroxide [48,49]. Lastly, we performed an assay using the Red Fluorometric ROS Kit, which generates a red-fluorescent product (λex = 520/λem = 605 nm) proportional to the presence of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals. The PL intensity at ∼525 nm is considerably increased when using SOSG (Fig. 6 a) and DHR 123 (Fig. S8c), indicating that our wt-CDs have the ability to generate singlet oxygen and hydrogen peroxide. Considering that UV radiation alone (control groups) is incapable of generating singlet oxygen (Fig. S8a) but produces a substantial amount of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. S8b), we can conclude that the presence of wt-CDs significantly enhances the generation of singlet oxygen compared to hydrogen peroxide. However, the PL intensity remains nearly unchanged when using the Red Fluorometric ROS Kit (Figs. S8d and e), suggesting that wt-CDs cannot generate superoxide or hydroxyl radicals. Therefore, we conclude that wt-CDs predominantly generate singlet oxygen and partially generate hydrogen peroxide under UV radiation, making them suitable for use as a PDT agent. According to previous research [43,50–54], the production of ROS by wt-CDs may be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., ketonic carbonyl groups) and chemically incomplete structures (e.g., dangling bonds and radicals).

Finally, we demonstrated apoptotic cell death induced by singlet oxygen generation to realize PDT based on wt-CDs. As direct UV radiation could cause damage and cell death, we employed a 473-nm two-photon laser. The effectiveness of PDT was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay and the control group in this experiment was not exposed to wt-CDs or laser irradiation. To determine the relationship between PDT efficacy and wt-CD concentration, PC3 cells were treated with wt-CDs at concentrations ranging from 0.125 to 1 mg mL−1. Cell viability was expressed relative to the control group, which was set at 100%. Notably, treatment with wt-CDs led to a significant reduction in cell viability, with the maximum decrease of approximately 20% observed at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 (Fig. 6b).

The effect of PDT was also confirmed by the live/dead assay. In the case of the control group, the green PL emission of live cells was observed. The experimental group exposed to laser radiation also exhibited mostly green PL emission similar to that of the control group (Fig. S9). With increasing wt-CD concentration, the intensities of green and red fluorescence decreased and increased, respectively (Fig. 6c). This behavior was consistent with the results described above (Fig. 6a and b) that indicated the central role of wt-CDs in ROS generation and the PDT effect. Taken together, wt-CDs were found to be a versatile and biocompatible theranostic agent.

In summary, we have successfully prepared wt-CDs from waste toner using a one-pot hydrothermal method with the aid of ethanolamine and analyzed the roles of this agent using XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. The amorphous carbon structures in wt-CDs originating from the CB of toner powder strongly interacted with UV (200–400 nm) light, which was manifested by intense PL around 300 and 450 nm. The ethanolamine-induced breakdown of the toner (especially the CB cores) resulted in the formation of surface defects, which led to the creation of defective states permitting ROS generation. Finally, as a proof of concept, we utilized the intense PL and ROS generation capability of wt-CDs to achieve the in vitro imaging and PDT of PC3 cells. Despite recognizing the existing limitations in terms of light absorption and PL emission, primarily within the UV–visible wavelength range, we firmly believe that our results offer a fresh recycling strategy for waste toner and exhibit its potential in biomedical applications.

Dajin Lee, Yoonsang Park, Yerim Byun, and Soohyun Cho manufactured the samples, performed the spectroscopy measurements, processed the experimental data, and designed the figures. Hyemin Kim and Hyunho Han performed the cell experiments and confocal microscopy measurements. Wonchan Park, Hyemin Kim, and Sei Kwang Hahn performed the photodynamic therapy experiments. Hyunho Han and Woosung Kwon conceived the original idea, directed the project, and wrote the manuscript with input from all authors. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403) of the National Research Foundation of Korea and by the Health Technology R&D Project (HI22C1186000022) of Korea Health Industry Development Institute (KHIDI) funded by the Ministry of Health & Welfare, Korea.
